title: Failed API/Login Attempt Detection

compliance.assurance_rationale: Detects repeated failed authentication attempts against the management system to support account lockout and response workflows.
compliance.controlObjective: Implements a detective control for patterns of unsuccessful logon or auth-related audit entries consistent with NIST 800-53 AC-7 expectations for the control plane when thresholds are policy-aligned.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Alert or report from `UC-5.13.48` with threshold, ticket ID for lockout, and runbook for credential abuse investigation.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Uses audit telemetry to flag potential malicious authentication activity against the management platform (system-level monitoring of security-relevant events).
compliance.controlObjective: Contributes to continuous monitoring of information-system security by watching authentication failure patterns in Catalyst Center logs.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Same alert, referenced in the SOC monitoring standard operating procedure for management-plane auth failures.

dataSources: `index=catalyst sourcetype=cisco:dnac:audit:logs`. from the `audit_logs` modular input in the Cisco Catalyst Add-on (Splunkbase 7538) polling `GET /dna/data/api/v1/event/event-series/audit-log`. Default poll interval: 300s (5m). Volume: Variable; ~10-100 events/day in typical environments. RBAC: SUPER-ADMIN-ROLE. Key fields: `auditUserName` (string), `auditRequestType` (string), `auditDescription` (string), `auditParentId` (string), `createdDateTime` (epoch milliseconds), `isSystemEvent` (boolean). CIM data model: Change.
spl: index=catalyst sourcetype="cisco:dnac:audit:logs" (auditRequestType="*LOGIN*" OR auditRequestType="*AUTH*") (auditDescription="*fail*" OR auditDescription="*denied*" OR auditDescription="*error*" OR auditDescription="*invalid*") | stats count as failed_attempts by auditUserName, auditIpAddress | where failed_attempts > 3 | sort -failed_attempts
description: Detects multiple failed login or API authentication attempts against Catalyst Center, which may indicate brute-force attacks or misconfigured integrations.
value: Failed authentication attempts are a primary indicator of credential attacks. Detecting them early enables account lockout and IP blocking before a breach occurs.
implementation: Enable the `audit_logs` input in the Cisco Catalyst TA pointing to `index=catalyst`. The TA polls audit log data from the Catalyst Center Intent API `/dna/intent/api/v1/audit/logs` every 5 minutes. Key fields: `auditRequestType`, `auditDescription`, `auditUserName`, `auditTimestamp`, `auditIpAddress`.
visualization: Table (auditUserName, auditIpAddress, failed_attempts), map of source IP if you enrich, alert when threshold is exceeded in a short window (use a shorter `stats` or `bin` in production).

references.title: Catalyst Center Audit Log API — Cisco DevNet

knownFalsePositives:
**Multiple administrators behind a shared NAT or jump host producing the same source IP.** Several administrators may share a single source IP address (VPN concentrator, jump host, bastion). A few honest password typos from different users can trigger the failed attempt threshold for that IP. Distinguish by checking whether the failed attempts come from different `username` values at the same `sourceIP`. Suppress by adjusting the detection threshold to per-username-per-IP rather than per-IP alone.

**API monitoring tool with expired or rotated credentials generating periodic failures.** Monitoring tools or scripts that have not been updated after an API key rotation will fail authentication on every poll cycle, generating a steady stream of failures. Distinguish by checking whether the failures come from a consistent `username` at regular intervals. Suppress by maintaining a `catalyst_service_accounts` lookup and filtering known automation accounts from the brute-force detection, while alerting the API key owner.

**LDAP/RADIUS integration timeout causing transient authentication failures.** If the external authentication server (LDAP, RADIUS) is temporarily slow or unreachable, legitimate login attempts may fail. These will resolve when the authentication server recovers. Distinguish by checking whether the failures correlate with authentication server health metrics or are concentrated in a short time window followed by successful logins. Suppress by requiring sustained failures (5+ consecutive from the same source within 10 minutes) before alerting.

**Catalyst Center SSO configuration change causing temporary login failures.** After an SSO configuration update (certificate rotation, IdP URL change), users may experience brief login failures until the new configuration propagates. Distinguish by correlating with `index=catalyst sourcetype="cisco:dnac:audit:logs"` for SSO configuration changes. Suppress by allowing a 30-minute grace period after SSO configuration changes.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Cisco Catalyst Add-on for Splunk (Splunkbase 7538) with the **audit_logs** input enabled to `index=catalyst`, `sourcetype=cisco:dnac:audit:logs`.
• RBAC: TA service account with **SUPER-ADMIN-ROLE** to access audit log API.
• Define failure detection thresholds: how many failed attempts within what time window constitute a security event. Common default: 5 failures from the same source within 10 minutes.

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Failed login and API attempts are recorded in the Catalyst Center audit log. The TA polls the audit log API at the configured interval.

Key fields for failed attempt detection:
• `auditResourceType`: "LOGIN" or "API" for authentication events.
• `auditDescription`: contains "failed" or "unauthorized" for failed attempts.
• `username`: the account that attempted authentication.
• `sourceIP`: the IP address of the attempt (may be behind NAT/proxy).
• `auditRequestType`: typically POST for login attempts.
• `statusCode`: HTTP status code — 401 (Unauthorized) or 403 (Forbidden) indicate failures.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert

```spl
index=catalyst sourcetype="cisco:dnac:audit:logs" (auditDescription="*fail*" OR auditDescription="*unauthorized*" OR auditDescription="*denied*" OR statusCode=401 OR statusCode=403)
| stats count as failure_count dc(username) as attempted_users values(username) as usernames earliest(_time) as first_attempt latest(_time) as last_attempt by sourceIP
| eval attempt_window_min=round((last_attempt-first_attempt)/60,1)
| where failure_count >= 5
| sort -failure_count
```

Understanding this SPL:
• **`auditDescription="*fail*" OR ... OR statusCode=401`**: Casts a wide net for failed authentication events. Catalyst Center logs failures with varying description formats across versions. The statusCode filter catches API authentication failures that may not have descriptive text.
• **`stats ... by sourceIP`**: Groups failures by source IP rather than username. This catches credential stuffing attacks where multiple usernames are tried from the same source.
• **`dc(username) as attempted_users`**: Counts distinct usernames attempted from each source IP. A high `attempted_users` count with many failures indicates credential stuffing or enumeration.
• **`attempt_window_min`**: Calculates the time span of the attack in minutes. Rapid failures (many attempts in <5 minutes) are more likely automated.
• **`where failure_count >= 5`**: Threshold for alerting — tune based on your environment's normal failure rate.

Step 3 — Validate
• **Test event:** Intentionally fail 5+ login attempts to the Catalyst Center UI or API from a known IP. Verify the failures appear in Splunk with the correct `sourceIP`, `username`, and `auditDescription`.
• **Field validation:** Run `| fieldsummary sourceIP` — if `sourceIP` is rarely populated, the audit log version may use a different field name. Check for `clientIp`, `remoteAddress`, or similar fields.

Step 4 — Operationalize
• **Dashboard:** Failed attempt heatmap by `sourceIP` and time, top attacker IPs table, and a timechart of failure rate trends.
• **Alert:** Trigger when `failure_count >= 10` from a single `sourceIP` within 15 minutes. Route to the security team. Throttle per `sourceIP` for 1 hour.
• **SIEM integration:** Forward failed login events to the SIEM for correlation with other authentication failures across the enterprise.

Step 5 — Troubleshoot
• **Shared NAT/VPN IP triggering false alerts:** Multiple administrators behind the same VPN concentrator may trigger the threshold. Check `dc(username)` — if multiple different users failed from the same IP, adjust the threshold or switch to per-username detection.
• **API monitoring tool generating failures:** An automation tool with expired credentials generates regular failures. Add the tool's `username` to a `catalyst_service_accounts` lookup and filter from the brute-force detection.
• **`sourceIP` not populated:** Some Catalyst Center versions do not include the source IP in all audit log entries. Upgrade the Catalyst Center or use alternative fields for detection.
• **No audit log events arriving:** verify the `audit_logs` input is enabled and check `index=_internal` for API error or endpoint timeout messages from the TA.
• **Missing fields or empty values:** some audit events from system processes have minimal descriptions; NULL values in `auditDescription` are expected for internal events.
• **Fewer events than expected:** the audit log API requires SUPER-ADMIN-ROLE; credential access denied errors indicate the service account lacks this role.
grandmaExplanation: We catch repeated failed login attempts to Catalyst Center, which could mean someone is trying to break in or a system has the wrong password.
